## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Yes, I can see it clearly. It's classic mojibake — wherever the diagram should show an **em dash (—)**, it instead shows `â€` (with a trailing glyph that's not rendering). It appears in five places:

- Title: `Federation Tier â€  Operator Login`
- Divider: `Leg 1 â€  IdP auth`
- Divider: `Leg 2 â€  STS exchange`
- Divider: `Downstream â€  model-blind`
- Footer note: `Session cap 15 min â€  12 h`

The cause is an encoding mismatch: a UTF-8 em dash (`—`, bytes `E2 80 94`) is being decoded as Windows-1252, which renders those three bytes as `â`, `€`, and `"` (the last being the invisible/garbled trailing character). So this is almost certainly in the committed PlantUML source — either the `.puml` file was saved with the wrong encoding, or PlantUML is being invoked without `-charset UTF-8`.

Want me to find the source `.puml` for `federation-login.svg` and confirm which it is? I can see whether the dashes are stored correctly (and it's a render-flag problem) or already corrupted in the file.

lastPrompt: find the source puml and confirm which it is.  I muse we need to make a PCG for PlantUml Coding Guide like our other ?CG documents to forbid this by constraint.

content.text:
Confirmed — and it's the better of the two outcomes: **the source is clean, the corruption is introduced at render time.** The evidence chain:

- **Source `.puml`** — hexdump of the title shows `Tier ` + `e2 80 94` + ` Operator`. That `E2 80 94` is a textbook UTF-8 em dash. All four `.puml` files are clean UTF-8; zero mojibake bytes present.
- **Rendered SVG** — the same dash is baked in as `â€…` (i.e. `â` `€` …), the Latin-1 misreading of those three UTF-8 bytes, emitted as XML numeric entities.

So PlantUML read the UTF-8 source as the platform default charset (Latin-1/CP1252) and froze the mojibake into the SVG. The fix is one flag on the render invocation: **`-charset UTF-8`** (equivalently `-Dfile.encoding=UTF-8`).

One honest gap: I confirmed *where* the corruption enters (render step) but I haven't located the actual render command — the README says the SVGs are rendered "through the pluml Crucible," and my grep didn't turn up a render tabtarget. These SVGs may have been generated by an ad-hoc invocation rather than a committed script. I can go find (or confirm the absence of) the render path next.

---

On the PCG — let me think with you rather than just draft it, because there are two real forks here.

**First fork: guideline vs. gate.** The whole `?CG` ethos is "forbid by constraint," and a *document* is the weakest form of that — a future instance can forget a guideline. The strongest, most BCG-faithful version is mechanical: pass `-charset UTF-8` in the one canonical render invocation (the membrane), *and* add a qualify check that fails if any committed SVG contains the `â€` signature (or if a `.puml` carries non-ASCII that wasn't deliberately allowed). A gate in `rbw-tq`/`rbw-tl` cannot be forgotten the way prose can. If we only write a PCG and don't fix the invocation + add a check, we've documented the cliff without building the fence.

**Second fork: does PCG earn a full sibling doc yet?** Here I want to flag a molehill risk honestly. BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG each govern a *rich* environment with many failure modes. PlantUML today is 4 diagram files with a narrow failure surface — right now the entire PCG would be roughly one rule (charset). That's a candidate for over-building.

But there's a framing that *would* earn it, and I think it's the right one: PlantUML is a **Palisade neighbor** (per the CMK ROE) — we can't edit its charset-defaulting, we can only conduct ourselves well at the edge. A PCG framed as *Palisade conduct for the diagram-rendering boundary* (characterize the exact signature, contain at one membrane = the render call, fail fast / gate on everything else) is principled rather than padded, and the charset rule is its seed crystal as more diagram discipline accretes.

My read: do the mechanical fix + gate now regardless (that's the load-bearing part), and let the PCG crystallize around it — either born now as a deliberately-small Palisade-conduct doc, or deferred until there's a second rule to keep the first company. I lean slightly toward seeding the doc now *because* you have the context loaded, but I don't want to rush it — which fork feels right to you?

message.content: find the render command first but yes we should seed PCG in this chat.

content.text: The render path is an HTTP POST to the PlantUML server (`/svg/uml`), not a CLI invocation — so the membrane is the POST, and the fix is the request charset, not a `-charset` flag. Let me read the POST helper to see exactly what gets sent.

lastPrompt: find the render command first but yes we should seed PCG in this chat.

content.text:
Render command found and root cause fully confirmed.

**The render command** is a theurge fixture case — `rbtdrc_pluml_render_diagrams` at `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2298`. It's not a CLI invocation of the PlantUML jar; it POSTs each `.puml` body to the live PlantUML *server* running in the `pluml` crucible:

```
curl -s --data-binary @-  http://localhost:{port}/svg/uml
```

(via the helper `rbtdrc_curl_post_stdin`, line 1769). The body is written with `body.as_bytes()` — i.e. **correct UTF-8 bytes go out on the wire**. I verified that end of it.

**So the corruption is on the server's *decode*, not our encode.** The chain, now complete:
- We send the em dash as `E2 80 94` (valid UTF-8). ✓
- The POST declares no charset (`curl --data-binary` defaults to `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, no charset parameter).
- The PlantUML server, getting no charset, decodes the body as the servlet default **ISO-8859-1** → reads those 3 bytes as `â € "` → bakes them into the SVG as `â€…` entities.

**The membrane** is that one POST. The fix is to declare the body charset on the request — `-H 'Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8'`. One honest caveat I won't paper over: that the server *honors* a request-charset header is standard servlet behavior but I haven't verified it against this specific PlantUML server image — confirming requires charging the `pluml` crucible and re-rendering. That live check is the validation step (operator-triggered). This is textbook Palisade conduct, which makes it a clean founding example for the guide.

---

**Before I seed the guide — a name collision I have to flag.** "PCG" is already taken: it maps to `bpu-PCG-ProcedureCurationGuide-005.md` in the CNMP lenses dir (it's in your CLAUDE.md acronym table). Minting a second PCG would violate the very uniqueness discipline the guide family is supposed to embody. So we need a different acronym.

Plan for the seed (once the name's settled): live at `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/<NAME>-…md` alongside its sibling CBG (RBK owns the render path + the diagrams). Founding rule framed as Palisade conduct for the PlantUML-render boundary: characterize the ISO-8859-1 decode, contain it at the single POST membrane, declare `charset=utf-8`, log the bend with a removal condition. The charset rule is the seed crystal.

The name is genuinely yours to call:

questions.question: "PCG" collides with the existing Procedure Curation Guide in the lenses dir. What acronym should the PlantUML guide take instead?

options.label: PUG
options.description: "PlantUml Guide." 3 letters — matches the sibling length (BCG/RCG/CBG/WSG are all 3). Memorable gestalt anchor, but drops the explicit "Coding" word.

options.label: PUCG
options.description: "PlantUml Coding Guide." Preserves your "...CG" intent exactly — only swaps the one colliding leading letter. 4 letters, so it stands out next to CBG in the dir.

options.label: DCG
options.description: "Diagram Coding Guide." Broader scope than PlantUML specifically — would be the home for any future diagram-tool discipline, not just PlantUML. Risks premature generalization (only PlantUML is in play today).

=== CBG header / opening (style + rule-family scheme) ===
<!--
Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
See LICENSE for terms.
-->

# Cloud Build Guide (CBG) — Step-Body Discipline

## Purpose

CBG codifies the discipline for authoring the **body of a single Google Cloud
Build step** — the script that runs inside one step's builder container. It is a
foreign-environment sibling to BCG (host bash) and RCG (host Rust): it shares
their philosophy — crash-fast, no silent failures, load-bearing complexity — but
diverges entirely on mechanics, because a step body executes in an environment
with none of the host kit's scaffolding.

CBG governs **single-step precision**: what goes inside one step's script. The
one seam it also covers is **how a step body is composed and dispatched** (the
include splice, the substitution contract, the return channel), because a body
must be authored *against* that machinery.

CBG is **polyglot**. A step body is bash, POSIX `sh`, or `python3` — the recipe
selects the interpreter per step. The cloud-step disciplines are
language-neutral; each language honors them with its own idiom.

## How this document is organized — two genres on purpose

CBG deliberately separates two kinds of knowledge, because they earn their form
differently:

- **Authored Disciplines** (prose, no IDs) — systematic, interderivable patterns
  for code *we* write. Like BCG, these are internalized, not cited by number;
  numbering them would be ceremony with no citers.
- **Cited Rules** (numbered `CB*-`) — discrete facts about the cloud Palisade we do
  *not* control (surprises, divergences, a recorded gap), each of which
  something will *point at*: a step comment justifying a workaround, a memo
  recording a probe, a review flagging a violation, or the dispatch validator.
  An ID earns its existence only when a citer will exist.

This split is itself an instance of the load-bearing test (BCG Core Philosophy):
catalog what is foreign; systematize what is yours.

## Core Philosophy

**The step body has no kit runtime.** On the host, BCG code leans on `buc_die`,
`buc_step`, the kindle/sentinel lifecycle, `buv_*` validation, and the module/CLI
gateway. **None of that exists inside a step.** A step body is a single file
shipped into a vendor builder container and run with a bare interpreter. Every
guarantee BCG gets from BUK, a step body must re-establish in a few lines of raw
bash or stdlib python. The philosophy survives the crossing; the mechanics are
rebuilt minimally, in-language. This is the relationship WSG has to BCG: the same
discipline, re-expressed for an environment BCG's helpers cannot reach.

**Crash-fast is non-negotiable, and louder here.** A step's stdout/stderr *is*
the Cloud Build log — the only forensic surface. There is no transcript file, no
`buc_log_*` sink, no temp directory to inspect afterward. A failure that does not
print and exit non-zero is a failure that vanishes. Every fallible operation dies
loudly, at its location, with a message naming what failed.

**The vendor is at the Palisade.** Cloud Build, the metadata server, the builder
images, and the substitution machinery are not ours to edit (Rules of
Engagement). We characterize their behavior precisely, contain our dependence at
named seams, and never paper over it. The Cited Rules below *are* that
characterization.

**Load-bearing complexity still rules.** Reuse only the irreducibly identical
core; leave kind-specific assembly in the step (RBSCJ composition rationale).

## The Cloud Step Environment

A step body runs under conditions a host script never faces. Each is a fault
domain — the source of most Cited Rules below.

| Condition | Consequence for the body |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Substitutions arrive as environment variables** (`_RBGx_*`, expanded at submit via `automapSubstitutions`). | Read from the environment, not argv; they may be empty (CBi_101). |
| **`/workspace` is a shared, ephemeral mount** seen by every step, destroyed at build end. | The only inter-step channel; non-secret by invariant; guard every read (CBi_102). |
| **Steps can be retried silently** on transient failure. | Every state change must be idempotent (CBi_103). |
| **Authentication is ambient**, via the metadata server. | Tokens are fetched in-memory, used, discarded — never to `/workspace` (CBi_102). |
| **The interpreter is the builder image's, pinned** (e.g. `gcloud` ships python 3.10). | Code to the shipped runtime; guard newer features (CBi_104). |
| **No native step reuse** (no anchors, no includes). | Shared bash logic is composed in on the host before submit (CBh_101). |

---

## Authored Disciplines (prose — internalize, don't cite)

### Crash-fast, spelled per language

The step body re-implements BCG's die-at-point-of-failure without BUK.

# Cloud Build Guide (CBG) — Step-Body Discipline

## Purpose

CBG codifies the discipline for authoring the **body of a single Google Cloud
Build step** — the script that runs inside one step's builder container. It is a
foreign-environment sibling to BCG (host bash) and RCG (host Rust): it shares
their philosophy — crash-fast, no silent failures, load-bearing complexity — but
diverges entirely on mechanics, because a step body executes in an environment
with none of the host kit's scaffolding.

CBG governs **single-step precision**: what goes inside one step's script. The
one seam it also covers is **how a step body is composed and dispatched** (the
include splice, the substitution contract, the return channel), because a body
must be authored *against* that machinery.

CBG is **polyglot**. A step body is bash, POSIX `sh`, or `python3` — the recipe
selects the interpreter per step. The cloud-step disciplines are
language-neutral; each language honors them with its own idiom.

## How this document is organized — two genres on purpose

CBG deliberately separates two kinds of knowledge, because they earn their form
differently:

- **Authored Disciplines** (prose, no IDs) — systematic, interderivable patterns
  for code *we* write. Like BCG, these are internalized, not cited by number;
  numbering them would be ceremony with no citers.
- **Cited Rules** (numbered `CB*-`) — discrete facts about the cloud Palisade we do
  *not* control (surprises, divergences, a recorded gap), each of which
  something will *point at*: a step comment justifying a workaround, a memo
  recording a probe, a review flagging a violation, or the dispatch validator.
  An ID earns its existence only when a citer will exist.

This split is itself an instance of the load-bearing test (BCG Core Philosophy):
catalog what is foreign; systematize what is yours.

## Core Philosophy

**The step body has no kit runtime.** On the host, BCG code leans on `buc_die`,

**Crash-fast is non-negotiable, and louder here.** A step's stdout/stderr *is*
the Cloud Build log — the only forensic surface. There is no transcript file, no

**The vendor is at the Palisade.** Cloud Build, the metadata server, the builder

named seams, and never paper over it. The Cited Rules below *are* that
characterization.

**Load-bearing complexity still rules.** Reuse only the irreducibly identical
core; leave kind-specific assembly in the step (RBSCJ composition rationale).

## The Cloud Step Environment

| **Substitutions arrive as environment variables** (`_RBGx_*`, expanded at submit via `automapSubstitutions`). | Read from the environment, not argv; they may be empty (CBi_101). |
| **`/workspace` is a shared, ephemeral mount** seen by every step, destroyed at build end. | The only inter-step channel; non-secret by invariant; guard every read (CBi_102). |
| **Steps can be retried silently** on transient failure. | Every state change must be idempotent (CBi_103). |
| **Authentication is ambient**, via the metadata server. | Tokens are fetched in-memory, used, discarded — never to `/workspace` (CBi_102). |
| **The interpreter is the builder image's, pinned** (e.g. `gcloud` ships python 3.10). | Code to the shipped runtime; guard newer features (CBi_104). |
| **No native step reuse** (no anchors, no includes). | Shared bash logic is composed in on the host before submit (CBh_101). |

content.content:
1	# Guide: Crafting Durable IT Procedures
2	
3	## Your Role as Digital Mind
4	
5	You're helping an Editor shape procedures that survive time and execute reliably. These procedures run infrequently - during incidents, quarterly maintenance, annual audits. The Executors are intelligent but unfamiliar with the specific task. Your job: make procedures they'll actually follow.
6	
7	The Editor trusts your judgment. When they review your work, they're checking outcomes, not scrutinizing every decision. Apply this guide with confidence.
8	
9	## The Core Insight
10	
11	Procedures fail when Executors deviate. They deviate when overwhelmed by words or starved of context. Every line must earn its presence by either preventing failure or enabling future repair.
12	
13	## The Guillemet Contract
14	
15	The `«...»` notation marks the boundary between layers:
16	
17	**In descriptive text:** Names for contexts and sessions
18	```markdown
19	Open terminal «PROD-DB» with production database access
20	```
21	
22	**In code blocks:** Values requiring substitution
23	```bash
24	export BKUP_BUCKET=prod-backups-2024
25	export BKUP_KEY=«aws-access-key-from-vault»
26	```
27	
28	This boundary is sacred. Never leave guillemets in final executable blocks.
29	
30	## Environment Variables as Memory
31	
32	Group configuration at the start with memorable prefixes:
33	
34	```bash
35	# PGMG = PostgreSQL Migration
36	export PGMG_SOURCE_HOST=db-prod.internal
37	export PGMG_TARGET_HOST=db-staging.internal
38	export PGMG_DUMP_FILE=/tmp/migration-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql
39	```
40	
41	Choose prefixes that are 3-5 characters, pronounceable, and unique to this procedure.
42	
43	## Command Construction Philosophy
44	
45	**Simple operations stand alone:**
46	```bash
47	kubectl get nodes
48	```
49	
50	**Critical chains demand propagation:**
51	```bash
52	pg_dump -h ${PGMG_SOURCE_HOST} > ${PGMG_DUMP_FILE} && \
53	psql -h ${PGMG_TARGET_HOST} < ${PGMG_DUMP_FILE} && \
54	echo "Migration complete"
55	```
56	
57	**Dangerous operations wear warnings:**
58	```markdown
59	⚠️ This drops all tables in the target database
60	```
61	```bash
62	psql -h ${PGMG_TARGET_HOST} -c "DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE"
63	```
64	
65	## Verification: Only When Uncertainty Lurks
66	
67	Include verification when:
68	- Success is silent but critical
69	- The operation takes >30 seconds (so Executors know it's still running)
70	- The next step depends on specific output
71	- Multiple commands are chained in one block
72	
73	Skip verification when the command's output speaks for itself.
74	
75	**Duration as upper bound:**
76	```bash
77	# Build image (fails if >5 minutes)
78	timeout 300 docker build -t ${IMG_NAME}:${IMG_TAG} .
79	```
80	
81	## The Single Path Principle
82	
83	When branches emerge in your draft:
84	1. If handling an edge case → remove it
85	2. If both paths are common → split into separate procedures
86	3. If organizational policy dictates → document only the blessed path
87	
88	The Executor should never choose. The procedure chooses for them.
89	
90	## Web UI: Intent Over Mechanics
91	
92	Capture what matters, flex on navigation:
93	
94	```markdown
95	1. AWS Console → RDS → Create database
96	2. Configure:
97	   - Engine: PostgreSQL 15.4
98	   - Template: Production (not Free Tier)
99	   - Instance: db.r6g.xlarge
100	3. Under "Additional configuration":
101	   - Initial database name: `appdb`
102	   - Backup retention: 7 days
103	```
104	
105	Record defaults only when they affect troubleshooting or cost.
106	
107	## Secret Management
108	
109	Always mark secrets for replacement:
110	```bash
111	export GHUB_TOKEN=«github-pat-with-repo-scope»
112	# Generate at: Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens
113	```
114	
115	Never put placeholder secrets that look real.
116	
117	## Your Compass Questions
118	
119	As you work, ask yourself:
120	
121	1. **Would an intelligent but stressed engineer follow this exactly?**
122	2. **If this fails at 3am, can someone debug it from the error alone?**
123	3. **When AWS changes their UI next quarter, will the intent still guide?**
124	4. **Have I made the Executor think, or just execute?**
125	
126	## Patterns That Emerge
127	
128	**Compound operations get single verification:**
129	```bash
130	terraform init && \
131	terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan && \
132	terraform apply plan.tfplan && \
133	echo "Infrastructure updated"
134	```
135	
136	**Long operations get timeouts:**
137	```bash
138	timeout 600 ansible-playbook -i prod deploy.yml
139	```
140	
141	**Defaults that matter get documented:**
142	```markdown
143	Leave "Enable DNS hostnames" at default (Yes) - required for our VPC endpoints
144	```
145	
146	**Context switches get announced:**
147	```markdown
148	Return to terminal «LOCAL-DEV» for the next steps
149	```
150	
151	## Trust Your Instincts
152	
153	When this guide doesn't address something:
154	- Prefer removing words over adding them
155	- Trust that Executors are intelligent
156	- Surface genuine ambiguity to the Editor
157	- Remember: procedures that get followed are better than perfect procedures
158	
159	The Editor will guide you on whether to use your training or search for documentation. Don't self-limit here.
160	
161	## The Health Check
162	
163	A procedure has achieved health when:
164	- An unfamiliar engineer can execute it without deviation
165	- Failures produce clear, actionable errors
166	- Environment changes require minimal updates
167	- Reading it feels like following a trail, not solving a puzzle
168	
169	You'll feel it when a procedure reaches this state. Trust that feeling.
170

export PGMG_DUMP_FILE=/tmp/migration-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql
```

Choose prefixes that are 3-5 characters, pronounceable, and unique to this procedure.

## Command Construction Philosophy

**Simple operations stand alone:**
```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

**Critical chains demand propagation:**
```bash
pg_dump -h ${PGMG_SOURCE_HOST} > ${PGMG_DUMP_FILE} && \
psql -h ${PGMG_TARGET_HOST} < ${PGMG_DUMP_FILE} && \
echo "Migration complete"
```

**Dangerous operations wear warnings:**
```markdown
⚠️ This drops all tables in the target database
```
```bash
psql -h ${PGMG_TARGET_HOST} -c "DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE"
```

## Verification: Only When Uncertainty Lurks

Include verification when:
- Success is silent but critical
- The operation takes >30 seconds (so Executors know it's still running)
- The next step depends on specific output
- Multiple commands are chained in one block

Skip verification when the command's output speaks for itself.

**Duration as upper bound:**
```bash
# Build image (fails if >5 minutes)
timeout 300 docker build -t ${IMG_NAME}:${IMG_TAG} .
```

## The Single Path Principle

When branches emerge in your draft:
1. If handling an edge case → remove it
2. If both paths are common → split into separate procedures
3. If organizational policy dictates → document only the blessed path

The Executor should never choose. The procedure chooses for them.

## Web UI: Intent Over Mechanics

Capture what matters, flex on navigation:

```markdown
1. AWS Console → RDS → Create database
2. Configure:
   - Engine: PostgreSQL 15.4
   - Template: Production (not Free Tier)
   - Instance: db.r6g.xlarge
3. Under "Additional configuration":
   - Initial database name: `appdb`
   - Backup retention: 7 days
```

Record defaults only when they affect troubleshooting or cost.

## Secret Management

Always mark secrets for replacement:
```bash
export GHUB_TOKEN=«github-pat-with-repo-scope»
# Generate at: Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens
```

Never put placeholder secrets that look real.

## Your Compass Questions

As you work, ask yourself:

1. **Would an intelligent but stressed engineer follow this exactly?**
2. **If this fails at 3am, can someone debug it from the error alone?**
3. **When AWS changes their UI next quarter, will the intent still guide?**
4. **Have I made the Executor think, or just execute?**

## Patterns That Emerge

**Compound operations get single verification:**
```bash
terraform init && \
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan && \
terraform apply plan.tfplan && \
echo "Infrastructure updated"
```

**Long operations get timeouts:**
```bash
timeout 600 ansible-playbook -i prod deploy.yml
```

**Defaults that matter get documented:**
```markdown
Leave "Enable DNS hostnames" at default (Yes) - required for our VPC endpoints
```

**Context switches get announced:**
```markdown
Return to terminal «LOCAL-DEV» for the next steps
```

## Trust Your Instincts

When this guide doesn't address something:
- Prefer removing words over adding them
- Trust that Executors are intelligent
- Surface genuine ambiguity to the Editor
- Remember: procedures that get followed are better than perfect procedures

The Editor will guide you on whether to use your training or search for documentation. Don't self-limit here.

## The Health Check

A procedure has achieved health when:
- An unfamiliar engineer can execute it without deviation
- Failures produce clear, actionable errors
- Environment changes require minimal updates
- Reading it feels like following a trail, not solving a puzzle

You'll feel it when a procedure reaches this state. Trust that feeling.

# POSTing UTF-8 to PlantUML Server

Based on the Q&A content provided, there is **no explicit specification** for the Content-Type header or charset requirements when POSTing to the plantuml-server.

The user reported an encoding issue: "a POST with Queen's renders properly with a GET but renders as Queenâ€™s with a POST." Their Python code used `requests.post(url, data=uml.encode('utf-8'))`, yet experienced mojibake despite manual UTF-8 encoding.

**Key observations:**
- The PlantUML server itself reports "Default Encoding: UTF-8" in its version info
- GET requests work correctly with UTF-8 characters
- POST requests exhibit character corruption despite encoding

**What's missing:** The forum post does not provide an answer specifying required headers. To properly POST UTF-8 content, you would typically need to set:
```
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
```

However, this specific guidance is **not present in the provided content**. The related question "Example on how to encode For PlantUML server?" is listed but its answer isn't included here. That resource may contain the definitive answer to your charset question.

content.content:
# PlantUML Text Encoding for URLs

## Encoding Process

PlantUML encodes diagram source text through a three-step process:

1. **UTF-8 encoding** of the text
2. **Deflate compression** (the default algorithm)
3. **ASCII re-encoding** using a custom character mapping

As the documentation states: "the text diagram is: 1. Encoded in UTF-8 2. Compressed using Deflate algorithm 3. Reencoded in ASCII using a transformation close to base64"

## Character Safety

The encoding scheme uses only "digits, letters, underscore and minus character," making it URL-safe and independent of HTTP charset negotiation. The custom alphabet differs from standard Base64:

- **PlantUML mapping:** `0123456789ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz-_`

This charset avoids problematic URL characters like `+`, `/`, and `=`.

## POST Requirements

The documentation does not address POSTing raw text or specific POST charset requirements. It focuses on URL encoding via the server endpoint and command-line encoding tools (`-encodeurl` / `-decodeurl` flags).

## Metadata Preservation

Notably, "the encoded metadata is stored in the generated PNG, so the diagram source can be extracted from the diagram itself," enabling round-trip encoding/decoding.

# PlantUml Guide (PUG) — Diagram Source & Render Discipline

## Purpose

PUG codifies the discipline for the project's PlantUML diagrams: how the
committed `.puml` sources are authored, and how they are rendered to the
committed `.svg` siblings linked from `README.md`. It is a foreign-environment
sibling to CBG (Cloud Build step bodies) and WSG (ssh-to-Windows transport),
sharing the BCG/RCG philosophy — crash-fast, no silent failures, load-bearing
complexity — but its mechanics are dominated by one fact: **the renderer is not
ours.** PlantUML sits at the Palisade (CMK Rules of Engagement). We cannot edit
how it decodes our input; we can only conduct ourselves precisely at the
membrane where our text crosses into it.

## How this document is organized — two genres on purpose

Like CBG, PUG separates two kinds of knowledge:

- **Authored Disciplines** (prose, no IDs) — patterns for what *we* control: the
  `.puml` sources and the render path. Internalized, not cited.
- **Cited Rules** (numbered `PU*_`) — discrete facts about the PlantUML Palisade
  we do *not* control, each of which something points at (a render comment, a
  memo, a review flag, a gate). An ID earns its existence only when a citer will.

## Core Philosophy

**PlantUML is at the Palisade.** The renderer — here the PlantUML server in the
`pluml` crucible — is a neighbor we cannot legislate. Per the CMK ROE:
characterize the foreign behavior precisely, contain dependence at one membrane,
absorb only the surveyed signature, log the bend with a removal condition.

**Encode past the neighbor; don't trust its decode.** The strongest membrane
removes the foreign decision entirely rather than depending on the neighbor to
make it correctly. HTTP charset negotiation is such a decision: the robust render
path encodes UTF-8 source into charset-independent ASCII *before* transit
(PUr_101), so no charset is ever negotiated on the wire.

**The render is one membrane, and byte-stable.** Diagrams render through exactly
one path — the `pluml` crucible render case (`rbtdrc_pluml_render_diagrams`). No
ad-hoc rendering. Identical source yields byte-identical SVG, so source and SVG
commit in lockstep and a clean tree stays clean; a diff in a committed SVG means
a diff in its source, never render nondeterminism.

## Authored Disciplines (prose — internalize, don't cite)

### Sources are UTF-8; non-ASCII is deliberate

`.puml` sources are UTF-8. Every multibyte glyph (em dash `—`, typographic
quotes, arrows) is a hostage to the render transport (PUr_101). Prefer plain
ASCII in diagram text where it reads as well; when a multibyte glyph earns its
place, know it rides entirely on the render path getting its bytes across intact.

### Render through the charset-independent transport

Render via PlantUML's deflate-encoded URL form, not raw-text POST that leaves the
body charset to the server. The encoding (UTF-8 → deflate → URL-safe ASCII; see
`plantuml.com/text-encoding`) is *independent of HTTP charset negotiation* — that
is the whole point: it carries exact UTF-8 bytes through a neighbor that
otherwise mis-decodes them. See PUr_101 for the failure this avoids and the one
fallback.

### Enforcement is mechanical, not advisory

A guide a future instance can forget is the weak form of "forbid by constraint."
The intended mechanical arm (**not yet built**) is a gate that rejects any
committed SVG carrying the mojibake signature `â€` — the ISO-8859-1
misread of a UTF-8 multibyte lead. The render case already fails loud on a
non-SVG / syntax-error response; the charset gate extends that to
corrupt-but-well-formed output. Until it exists, this prose is the only fence.

## Cited Rules (numbered — each has a citer)

### PUr_ — PlantUML render transport (the server we don't control)

**PUr_101 — raw-text POST body decodes as ISO-8859-1 absent a charset.**
The PlantUML server's text endpoint (`POST /svg/uml`) decodes the request body
as the servlet default **ISO-8859-1** when the `Content-Type` carries no
`charset`. UTF-8 multibyte input is then read byte-by-byte as Latin-1 and frozen
into the SVG as per-byte numeric entities — a UTF-8 em dash (`E2 80 94`) becomes
`â€”` (`â€"`). We send correct UTF-8 bytes; the corruption is the
server's decode. Independently reproduced upstream (PlantUML forum: `Queen's` →
`Queenâ€™s` on POST while the same source renders cleanly via GET, even though the
server reports "Default Encoding: UTF-8").

- **Membrane (robust):** render through the deflate-encoded URL transport
  (UTF-8 → deflate → URL-safe ASCII; `plantuml.com/text-encoding`), which is
  *independent of HTTP charset negotiation*. This is the Palisade-correct fix —
  it encodes past the neighbor's decode rather than depending on it.
- **Fallback (cheaper, unverified):** set `Content-Type: text/plain;
  charset=utf-8` on the existing POST (`rbtdrc_pluml_render_diagrams` →
  `rbtdrc_curl_post_stdin`, `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). Servlet
  request-charset *may* be honored, but the upstream forum reproduces the bug
  without confirming a header fix, and it still rests on the server's decode path
  — so it must be live-verified (charge `pluml`, re-render, diff the SVG) before
  trust. Apply at the call site, never the shared POST helper, whose other
  callers must not be perturbed.
- **Removal condition:** the server's POST body decode defaults to UTF-8 — then
  the encode-past transport is no longer load-bearing for correctness (still fine
  to keep for URL-safety).

---

This document is a seed, founded on PUr_101 — the em-dash mojibake in the
federation-tier diagrams. Further PlantUML disciplines accrete here as found.

1	# Accord Builder API Guide
2	
3	## Document Context
4	
5	This guide implements WardRealm Substrate concepts defined in:
6	- `wrs-WRC-WardRealmConcepts.adoc` - Master concept definitions
7	- `mcm-MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc` - Documentation patterns
8	
9	Terms like Ward, Fief, Message, and Steward refer to formal definitions in the WRC document.
10	
11	## Type System
12	
13	### Forms
14	A Form is any type usable in properties, fields, and shadows. The type system supports the following Forms:
15	
16	**Primitive Forms**
17	- Signed integers: `I8`, `I16`, `I32`, `I64`
18	- Unsigned integers: `U8`, `U16`, `U32`, `U64`
19	- Floating point: `f32`, `f64`
20	- Other: `bool`, `char`
21	
22	**Constructed Forms**
23	- **DomainType**: Semantic wrapper around a Primitive
24	- **Enumeration**: Named symbolic values
25	- **Record**: Composite type containing typed Members
26	- **Array**: Elements of a single Form with size constraint
27	- **CoArray**: Multiple arrays sharing a common dynamic size
28	- **Index**: Type-safe array index derived from a Bound
29	
30	### Bounds
31	A Bound is not a Form, but a specification that creates Forms:
32	- Creates Array Forms when paired with an element type
33	- Creates CoArray Forms for synchronized array groups
34	- Creates Index Forms for type-safe array access
35	
36	### Type Creation Examples
37	
38	```python
39	# Primitive Form
40	builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32  # Form: U32
41	
42	# DomainType Form
43	session_id_t = builder.ab_domain('SessionId', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32,
44	                                 d="Unique session identifier")  # Form: DomainType
45	
46	# Enumeration Form
47	with builder.ab_enumeration('State', d="Connection lifecycle states") as state_enum:
48	    state_enum.ab_value('Active')
49	    state_enum.ab_value('Idle')
50	
51	# Record Form
52	with builder.ab_record('Point', d="2D coordinate") as point:
53	    point.ab_member('x', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Horizontal position")
54	    point.ab_member('y', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Vertical position")
55	
56	# Array Forms
57	simple_array = builder.ab_array(builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U8, max=256)  # Form: Array
58	capacity = builder.ab_bound('Capacity', max=1024, d="Maximum buffer size")
59	bounded_array = builder.ab_array(builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U8, capacity)  # Form: Array
60	
61	# CoArray Form
62	with builder.ab_coarray('Metrics', capacity, d="Synchronized metric arrays") as metrics:
63	    metrics.ab_array('values', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Measurement values")
64	    metrics.ab_array('timestamps', timestamp_t, d="When measured")
65	
66	# Harvest Form (defined within summons response)
67	with bracket.ab_message(response) as resp:
68	    with resp.ab_harvest(d="Per-reactor collected data") as harvest:
69	        harvest.ab_field('node_id', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32, d="Reporting node")
70	        harvest.ab_field('value', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Node's contribution")

# Accord Builder API Guide

## Document Context

This guide implements WardRealm Substrate concepts defined in:
- `wrs-WRC-WardRealmConcepts.adoc` - Master concept definitions
- `mcm-MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc` - Documentation patterns

Terms like Ward, Fief, Message, and Steward refer to formal definitions in the WRC document.

## Type System

### Forms
A Form is any type usable in properties, fields, and shadows. The type system supports the following Forms:

**Primitive Forms**
- Signed integers: `I8`, `I16`, `I32`, `I64`
- Unsigned integers: `U8`, `U16`, `U32`, `U64`
- Floating point: `f32`, `f64`
- Other: `bool`, `char`

**Constructed Forms**
- **DomainType**: Semantic wrapper around a Primitive
- **Enumeration**: Named symbolic values
- **Record**: Composite type containing typed Members
- **Array**: Elements of a single Form with size constraint
- **CoArray**: Multiple arrays sharing a common dynamic size
- **Index**: Type-safe array index derived from a Bound

### Bounds
A Bound is not a Form, but a specification that creates Forms:
- Creates Array Forms when paired with an element type
- Creates CoArray Forms for synchronized array groups
- Creates Index Forms for type-safe array access

### Type Creation Examples

```python
# Primitive Form
builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32  # Form: U32

# DomainType Form
session_id_t = builder.ab_domain('SessionId', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32,
                                 d="Unique session identifier")  # Form: DomainType

# Enumeration Form
with builder.ab_enumeration('State', d="Connection lifecycle states") as state_enum:
    state_enum.ab_value('Active')
    state_enum.ab_value('Idle')

# Record Form
with builder.ab_record('Point', d="2D coordinate") as point:
    point.ab_member('x', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Horizontal position")
    point.ab_member('y', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Vertical position")

# Array Forms
simple_array = builder.ab_array(builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U8, max=256)  # Form: Array
capacity = builder.ab_bound('Capacity', max=1024, d="Maximum buffer size")
bounded_array = builder.ab_array(builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U8, capacity)  # Form: Array

# CoArray Form
with builder.ab_coarray('Metrics', capacity, d="Synchronized metric arrays") as metrics:
    metrics.ab_array('values', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Measurement values")
    metrics.ab_array('timestamps', timestamp_t, d="When measured")

# Harvest Form (defined within summons response)
with bracket.ab_message(response) as resp:
    with resp.ab_harvest(d="Per-reactor collected data") as harvest:
        harvest.ab_field('node_id', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_U32, d="Reporting node")
        harvest.ab_field('value', builder.AB_PRIMITIVE_F32, d="Node's contribution")

## Character
Doc authoring from a design settled in conversation — transcription, not invention. The paddock carries the spine, the three-homes frame, the v1 posture, the two source-doc forms, and the heat's move discipline; this docket carries only the ACG's operational form.

## What the ACG is (operational form)
A catalog of mechanical, verifier-backed move-types — not a prose style guide. Each move states: detect-rule, authority (ground truth), mutate-now-or-detect-only, verifier, countable Done. The ACG codifies the heat's move discipline (see paddock) as this catalog; the three-homes frame stays the why.

## Seed the catalog
- magic-string to constant (proven via the rbrv sweep) — mutate-now.

## AXLA/MCM nudges in scope (deft, only if needed)

1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	All rights reserved.
4	SPDX-License-Identifier: LicenseRef-Proprietary
5	-->
6	
7	# Allocation Coding Guide (ACG) — Reference the Home
8	
9	## Purpose
10	
11	ACG codifies one discipline: **don't recreate inline what already has a named
12	home — reference the home.** It is the language-neutral, allocation-facing
13	sibling to BCG (host bash), RCG (host Rust), WSG (Windows transport), and CBG
14	(cloud step). Those govern *how* code in one environment is written; ACG governs
15	*where a fact or a name is allowed to live*, and what source must do when a home
16	already exists: point at it, never restate it.
17	
18	ACG is the **source-side complement to MCM**. MCM builds the named homes — quoins
19	in specs, constants in code; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather
20	than recreate them. That complement is why ACG lives in the CMK kit beside MCM
21	and AXLA rather than in any single-language kit.
22	
23	ACG is **v1**: it blesses and names emergent practice and states the spine. It
24	does **not** mandate universals ("every verb must announce"); those are
25	candidates, confirmed move-by-move, not law. There is no qualify-time enforcement
26	in v1. Every move-type below is licensed precisely — most are detect-and-report,
27	not repair.
28	
29	## How this document is organized — frame, then catalog
30	
31	ACG separates two kinds of knowledge, because they earn their form differently:
32	
33	- **The Frame** (prose, no IDs) — the spine, the three homes, the v1 posture, the
34	  move discipline. Systematic and interderivable; internalized, not cited by
35	  number.
36	- **The Move Catalog** (numbered `ACGm_`) — discrete, verifier-backed move-types,
37	  each of which something *points at*: a constant's breadcrumb comment, a
38	  detect-and-report verb, a conformance-engine row, a review flagging a
39	  violation. An ID earns its existence only when a citer will exist.
40	
41	This split is itself the load-bearing test (BCG Core Philosophy): catalog the
42	discrete moves; systematize the discipline behind them.
43	
44	## The spine — reference the home
45	
46	The governing idea, stated once:
47	
48	> Don't recreate inline what has a named home. **Values → constants; concepts →
49	> quoin-refs.** MCM builds the homes; ACG governs source's duty to reference
50	> rather than recreate.
51	
52	This is the **allocation instance of Load-Bearing Complexity** (CLAUDE.md). A
53	recreated value or name is a non-load-bearing duplicate: its existence adds drift
54	risk without adding correctness, and the drift it invites is exactly the gap
55	between intent and behavior the principle forbids. BCG's and RCG's Constant
56	Discipline and Interface Contamination are the per-language instances of this;
57	ACG is the general statement those instantiate, plus the one thing a
58	single-language guide cannot state — *where prose with no code-home actually
59	goes.*
60	
61	## The three homes — allocate by when-read
62	
63	A fact has exactly one right home, fixed by *when it is read*:
64	
65	| When read | Home | Carries |
66	|-----------|------|---------|
67	| **Design-time** | the spec (MCM quoin) | what a thing *is* and why it is shaped so — the conceptual model |
68	| **Edit-time** | the source comment | operational mechanics only — language idiom, and the Palisade (foreign-boundary) characterization a maintainer needs *at the code* |
69	| **Execution-time** | the runtime announcement | what is happening now — intent printed as the operation runs |
70	
71	Most conceptual comments are **temporal misallocation**: design-time or
72	execution-time knowledge dumped into the edit-time medium, where it rots for lack
73	of a forcing function. The spec is re-read when the design is questioned; the
74	announcement is re-read every run; an edit-time comment restating either is read
75	by no one with a reason to correct it when it drifts.
76	
77	Two edit-time/execution-time source-doc forms are distinct and **both blessed**:
78	
79	- **Contract header** — a bounded edit-time comment stating a function's input
80	  contract and invariants. Read when editing the function.
81	- **Intent announcement** — an execution-time printout naming what the operation
82	  is doing. Read when running it.
83	
84	Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-parameter-shows-the-options
85	practice, live in `rbfc_require_vessel_sigil`) is blessed alongside happy-path
86	announcement: an error that lists the valid forms is an execution-time
87	announcement of the contract, not contamination.
88	
89	## The move discipline — one litmus, three rules
90	
91	Every cleanup move obeys one litmus:
92	
93	> **Mutate only where a *wrong* move is cheaply caught; everywhere else the verb
94	> is detect-and-report, never repair.**
95	
96	Code-side moves — the value resolves through a named constant, and shellcheck
97	plus the fast suite verify it — may mutate now. Spec- and document-side moves are
98	detect-and-report until the lexer/linter that would catch a wrong move exists.
99	
100	Three rules ride that litmus:
101	
102	1. **Triage is part of every move, not an afterthought.** Exclude the
103	   false-positive class first; declare the authoritative side before any
104	   alignment; gate scope to settled lanes — never terms a hot heat is still
105	   moving. A sweep run against vocabulary two heats are still relocating only
106	   re-creates the drift it meant to kill.
107	2. **A checker proves itself against a known answer before its output is
108	   trusted.** The conformance fixture (next section) is the canonical home for
109	   such checks — ACG eating its own dog food: a named home, referenced not
110	   recreated.
111	3. **Concept linkage is name-identity.** The implementing symbol *is* the link: a
112	   function name equals its quoin's display-text, a tabtarget filename is the
113	   quoin's sprue, a scoped method is its inlay. A source-to-spec comment is the
114	   *exception*, reserved for where no single symbol can carry the link.
115	
116	## The named home — the conformance fixture
117	
118	Rule 2 and move-types ACGm_102/103 all defer to one concrete home: the
119	**`conformance`** fast-tier theurge fixture
120	(`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrn_conformance.rs`), registered in the `fast` suite. It
121	is to evicted vocabulary what BUK's `bug_require_clean_tree` is to the clean-tree
122	gate — the single named place the check lives, so no caller improvises its own.
123	
124	The fixture is a data-driven engine, not a word list. It reads rows of
125	`{kill_stem, keep_contexts}` and scans `Tools/` and `tt/` with identifier-boundary
126	awareness, flagging a stem as a bare token while sparing it inside a kept
127	identifier (`Identifier`) or under an exempt path (`PathPrefix`). A row with empty
128	`keep_contexts` is a *pure corpse* — the term must appear nowhere. The engine
129	proves itself against known inputs before its verdict on the live tree is trusted
130	(move discipline, rule 2), so it ships proven with zero production rows.
131	
132	**The rule: retire a term → add a row there. Never improvise a grep.** Population
133	is deliberately deferred — each term gains its row behind its own cutover, owned
134	by the heat retiring it — never against vocabulary a hot heat is still moving
135	(move discipline, rule 1).
136	
137	## The Move Catalog
138	
139	Each move-type states five things: **Detect** (the rule that finds a site),
140	**Authority** (the ground truth a site is judged against), **Licensing**,
141	**Verifier**, and a countable **Done**. Headers tag the licensing: **🔧
142	mutate-now** (a wrong move is cheaply caught — repair in place) or **🔍
143	detect-only** (read and report; no repair until a verifier exists). Numbered from
144	101 to leave room for insertion; once a move has a citer it is never renumbered.
145	
146	### 🔧 ACGm_101: magic-string → constant
147	
148	- **Detect:** a literal value — a path fragment, a filename, a repeated number or
149	  string — that *constructs or addresses* something, where a named home exists or
150	  should. Not human-facing prose.
151	- **Authority:** the value resolves through a single named constant home (e.g.
152	  the `RBCC_rbr*_file` family in `rbcc_Constants.sh`). The constant is ground
153	  truth; every literal is a copy that can drift.
154	- **Licensing:** mutate-now — the constant either resolves or the verifier fails,
155	  so a wrong move dies loud and immediately.
156	- **Verifier:** bash — `tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh` + `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh`
157	  green; Rust — `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` + `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh` green.
158	- **Done:** every genuine construction site resolves through the constant; the
159	  constant carries a breadcrumb to its spec quoin.
160	- **Triage:** naming a file *for a human* in a message is not a magic string —
161	  `doc_params`, `buc_info`/`buh_line` messages, and comments keep the literal.
162	  Exclude that class before sweeping.
163	- **Worked example:** the `rbrv.env` filename sweep is the first worked
164	  application of this move.
165	
166	### 🔍 ACGm_102: name-identity
167	
168	- **Detect:** an operation quoin (voiced `//axvo_procedure` or `//axvo_method`)
169	  whose display-text and implementing symbol disagree.
170	- **Authority:** the implementing symbol is the link (move discipline, rule 3) —
171	  a procedure's function name equals its quoin's display-text; a source-to-spec
172	  comment is the exception where no single symbol can carry it.
173	- **Licensing:** detect-only — document-side; no mutation until the lexer/linter
174	  exists.
175	- **Verifier:** none mechanized in v1 — read and reported by hand. The
176	  conformance fixture is the future verifier.
177	- **Done:** a report of operation-quoin / symbol disagreements; nothing mutated.
178	- **Formal dependency:** full mechanical decidability waits on an AXLA arity
179	  change — an explicit symbol lookahead on `axvo_procedure` / `axvo_method`
180	  (one `mcm_inlay`, the prefix-disciplined source symbol; mirroring
181	  `axvo_tabtarget`'s literal lookahead). That change carries a migration across
182	  every live operation voicing, so it is deferred to its own pace (see *The
183	  AXLA/MCM interface*). Until it lands, the move is read against the existing
184	  `axvo_*` voicings — which already mark *which* quoins are operations — plus the
185	  implementation symbol named at an operation's detail site (`axhems_scoped_method`).
186	
187	### 🔍 ACGm_103: typed-parameter lookahead
188	
189	- **Detect:** a parameter whose type is resolvable by bounded lookahead but is
190	  not yet carried as a type quoin-ref.
191	- **Authority:** the lookahead scanning rule (below) — the parameter's type is
192	  the attribute-reference the marker reads.
193	- **Licensing:** detect-only — document-side.
194	- **Verifier:** none in v1; the future lexer/linter.
195	- **Done:** a report of parameters whose type is derivable but unhomed; nothing
196	  mutated.
197	
198	## The AXLA/MCM interface
199	
200	### The lookahead scanning rule
201	
202	When counting a marker's lookahead in an MCM document, scan the following text and
203	**skip prose lines and blank lines; count only attribute-references (`{…}` linked
204	terms) and backtick sprues.** A dimension that counts "the Nth linked term" (e.g.
205	`axd_grouped`) counts attribute-references only — a backtick inlay or sprue is a
206	distinct token type and does not shift that count.
207	
208	### Name-identity's symbol-link dependency (deferred)
209	
210	ACGm_102 becomes mechanically decidable only when an operation voicing carries its
211	implementing symbol explicitly. The surveyed shape: add a one-`mcm_inlay`
212	lookahead to `axvo_procedure` / `axvo_method` reading the literal source symbol
213	(the MCM-correct token for a prefix-disciplined name, matching how
214	`axhems_scoped_method` already reads an implementation method name). It is an
215	*amendment* to two established voicings with many live consumers, not a fresh
216	mint, so it carries a real migration and is held for its own deliberate pace
217	rather than folded into guide authoring. Recorded here as ACGm_102's named
218	dependency; raised and deferred, not dropped.
219	
220	## Related Guides
221	
222	- **BCG** — host bash. Constant Discipline and Interface Contamination are the
223	  bash instances of the spine.
224	- **RCG** — host Rust. Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings), String Boundary
225	  Discipline, and Interface Contamination are the Rust instances.
226	- **WSG**, **CBG** — foreign-environment siblings (Windows transport, cloud step).
227	- **MCM** — builds the named homes ACG references; the design-time home.
228	- **AXLA** — the motif vocabulary; the lookahead scanning rule and the
229	  name-identity symbol-link dependency live against it.
230	- **CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" (mint)** — governs the naming homes
231	  themselves.
232	
233	## Acronym Registry
234	
235	| Term | Expansion |
236	|------|-----------|
237	| ACG | Allocation Coding Guide (this document) |
238	| The spine | "Reference the home" — values → constants, concepts → quoin-refs |
239	| Home | The one right location for a fact, fixed by when it is read (design / edit / execution time) |
240	| Move-type | A catalogued `ACGm_` cleanup move: detect-rule, authority, licensing, verifier, countable Done |
241	| mutate-now | Licensing: a wrong move is cheaply caught, so the verb may repair in place |
242	| detect-only | Licensing: the verb reads and reports; no repair until a verifier exists |
243	| Name-identity | Concept linkage where the implementing symbol *is* the link to its quoin |
244

# Allocation Coding Guide (ACG) — Reference the Home

## Purpose

ACG codifies one discipline: **don't recreate inline what already has a named
home — reference the home.** It is the language-neutral, allocation-facing
sibling to BCG (host bash), RCG (host Rust), WSG (Windows transport), and CBG
(cloud step). Those govern *how* code in one environment is written; ACG governs
*where a fact or a name is allowed to live*, and what source must do when a home
already exists: point at it, never restate it.

ACG is the **source-side complement to MCM**. MCM builds the named homes — quoins
in specs, constants in code; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather
than recreate them. That complement is why ACG lives in the CMK kit beside MCM
and AXLA rather than in any single-language kit.

ACG is **v1**: it blesses and names emergent practice and states the spine. It
does **not** mandate universals ("every verb must announce"); those are
candidates, confirmed move-by-move, not law. There is no qualify-time enforcement
in v1. Every move-type below is licensed precisely — most are detect-and-report,
not repair.

## How this document is organized — frame, then catalog

ACG separates two kinds of knowledge, because they earn their form differently:

- **The Frame** (prose, no IDs) — the spine, the three homes, the v1 posture, the
  move discipline. Systematic and interderivable; internalized, not cited by
  number.
- **The Move Catalog** (numbered `ACGm_`) — discrete, verifier-backed move-types,
  each of which something *points at*: a constant's breadcrumb comment, a
  detect-and-report verb, a conformance-engine row, a review flagging a
  violation. An ID earns its existence only when a citer will exist.

This split is itself the load-bearing test (BCG Core Philosophy): catalog the
discrete moves; systematize the discipline behind them.

## The spine — reference the home

The governing idea, stated once:

> Don't recreate inline what has a named home. **Values → constants; concepts →
> quoin-refs.** MCM builds the homes; ACG governs source's duty to reference
> rather than recreate.

This is the **allocation instance of Load-Bearing Complexity** (CLAUDE.md). A
recreated value or name is a non-load-bearing duplicate: its existence adds drift
risk without adding correctness, and the drift it invites is exactly the gap
between intent and behavior the principle forbids. BCG's and RCG's Constant
Discipline and Interface Contamination are the per-language instances of this;
ACG is the general statement those instantiate, plus the one thing a
single-language guide cannot state — *where prose with no code-home actually
goes.*

## The three homes — allocate by when-read

A fact has exactly one right home, fixed by *when it is read*:

| When read | Home | Carries |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Design-time** | the spec (MCM quoin) | what a thing *is* and why it is shaped so — the conceptual model |
| **Edit-time** | the source comment | operational mechanics only — language idiom, and the Palisade (foreign-boundary) characterization a maintainer needs *at the code* |
| **Execution-time** | the runtime announcement | what is happening now — intent printed as the operation runs |

Most conceptual comments are **temporal misallocation**: design-time or
execution-time knowledge dumped into the edit-time medium, where it rots for lack
of a forcing function. The spec is re-read when the design is questioned; the
announcement is re-read every run; an edit-time comment restating either is read
by no one with a reason to correct it when it drifts.

Two edit-time/execution-time source-doc forms are distinct and **both blessed**:

- **Contract header** — a bounded edit-time comment stating a function's input
  contract and invariants. Read when editing the function.
- **Intent announcement** — an execution-time printout naming what the operation
  is doing. Read when running it.

Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-parameter-shows-the-options
practice, live in `rbfc_require_vessel_sigil`) is blessed alongside happy-path
announcement: an error that lists the valid forms is an execution-time
announcement of the contract, not contamination.

## The move discipline — one litmus, three rules

Every cleanup move obeys one litmus:

> **Mutate only where a *wrong* move is cheaply caught; everywhere else the verb
> is detect-and-report, never repair.**

Code-side moves — the value resolves through a named constant, and shellcheck
plus the fast suite verify it — may mutate now. Spec- and document-side moves are
detect-and-report until the lexer/linter that would catch a wrong move exists.

Three rules ride that litmus:

1. **Triage is part of every move, not an afterthought.** Exclude the
   false-positive class first; declare the authoritative side before any
   alignment; gate scope to settled lanes — never terms a hot heat is still
   moving. A sweep run against vocabulary two heats are still relocating only
   re-creates the drift it meant to kill.
2. **A checker proves itself against a known answer before its output is
   trusted.** The conformance fixture (next section) is the canonical home for
   such checks — ACG eating its own dog food: a named home, referenced not
   recreated.
3. **Concept linkage is name-identity.** The implementing symbol *is* the link: a
   function name equals its quoin's display-text, a tabtarget filename is the
   quoin's sprue, a scoped method is its inlay. A source-to-spec comment is the
   *exception*, reserved for where no single symbol can carry the link.

## The named home — the conformance fixture

Rule 2 and move-types ACGm_102/103 all defer to one concrete home: the
**`conformance`** fast-tier theurge fixture
(`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrn_conformance.rs`), registered in the `fast` suite. It
is to evicted vocabulary what BUK's `bug_require_clean_tree` is to the clean-tree
gate — the single named place the check lives, so no caller improvises its own.

The fixture is a data-driven engine, not a word list. It reads rows of
`{kill_stem, keep_contexts}` and scans `Tools/` and `tt/` with identifier-boundary
awareness, flagging a stem as a bare token while sparing it inside a kept
identifier (`Identifier`) or under an exempt path (`PathPrefix`). A row with empty
`keep_contexts` is a *pure corpse* — the term must appear nowhere. The engine
proves itself against known inputs before its verdict on the live tree is trusted
(move discipline, rule 2), so it ships proven with zero production rows.

**The rule: retire a term → add a row there. Never improvise a grep.** Population
is deliberately deferred — each term gains its row behind its own cutover, owned
by the heat retiring it — never against vocabulary a hot heat is still moving
(move discipline, rule 1).

## The Move Catalog

Each move-type states five things: **Detect** (the rule that finds a site),
**Authority** (the ground truth a site is judged against), **Licensing**,
**Verifier**, and a countable **Done**. Headers tag the licensing: **🔧
mutate-now** (a wrong move is cheaply caught — repair in place) or **🔍
detect-only** (read and report; no repair until a verifier exists). Numbered from
101 to leave room for insertion; once a move has a citer it is never renumbered.

### 🔧 ACGm_101: magic-string → constant

- **Detect:** a literal value — a path fragment, a filename, a repeated number or
  string — that *constructs or addresses* something, where a named home exists or
  should. Not human-facing prose.
- **Authority:** the value resolves through a single named constant home (e.g.
  the `RBCC_rbr*_file` family in `rbcc_Constants.sh`). The constant is ground
  truth; every literal is a copy that can drift.
- **Licensing:** mutate-now — the constant either resolves or the verifier fails,
  so a wrong move dies loud and immediately.
- **Verifier:** bash — `tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh` + `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh`
  green; Rust — `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` + `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh` green.
- **Done:** every genuine construction site resolves through the constant; the
  constant carries a breadcrumb to its spec quoin.
- **Triage:** naming a file *for a human* in a message is not a magic string —
  `doc_params`, `buc_info`/`buh_line` messages, and comments keep the literal.
  Exclude that class before sweeping.
- **Worked example:** the `rbrv.env` filename sweep is the first worked
  application of this move.

### 🔍 ACGm_102: name-identity

- **Detect:** an operation quoin (voiced `//axvo_procedure` or `//axvo_method`)
  whose display-text and implementing symbol disagree.
- **Authority:** the implementing symbol is the link (move discipline, rule 3) —
  a procedure's function name equals its quoin's display-text; a source-to-spec
  comment is the exception where no single symbol can carry it.
- **Licensing:** detect-only — document-side; no mutation until the lexer/linter
  exists.
- **Verifier:** none mechanized in v1 — read and reported by hand. The
  conformance fixture is the future verifier.
- **Done:** a report of operation-quoin / symbol disagreements; nothing mutated.
- **Formal dependency:** full mechanical decidability waits on an AXLA arity
  change — an explicit symbol lookahead on `axvo_procedure` / `axvo_method`
  (one `mcm_inlay`, the prefix-disciplined source symbol; mirroring
  `axvo_tabtarget`'s literal lookahead). That change carries a migration across
  every live operation voicing, so it is deferred to its own pace (see *The
  AXLA/MCM interface*). Until it lands, the move is read against the existing
  `axvo_*` voicings — which already mark *which* quoins are operations — plus the
  implementation symbol named at an operation's detail site (`axhems_scoped_method`).

### 🔍 ACGm_103: typed-parameter lookahead

- **Detect:** a parameter whose type is resolvable by bounded lookahead but is
  not yet carried as a type quoin-ref.
- **Authority:** the lookahead scanning rule (below) — the parameter's type is
  the attribute-reference the marker reads.
- **Licensing:** detect-only — document-side.
- **Verifier:** none in v1; the future lexer/linter.
- **Done:** a report of parameters whose type is derivable but unhomed; nothing
  mutated.

## The AXLA/MCM interface

### The lookahead scanning rule

When counting a marker's lookahead in an MCM document, scan the following text and
**skip prose lines and blank lines; count only attribute-references (`{…}` linked
terms) and backtick sprues.** A dimension that counts "the Nth linked term" (e.g.
`axd_grouped`) counts attribute-references only — a backtick inlay or sprue is a
distinct token type and does not shift that count.

### Name-identity's symbol-link dependency (deferred)

ACGm_102 becomes mechanically decidable only when an operation voicing carries its
implementing symbol explicitly. The surveyed shape: add a one-`mcm_inlay`
lookahead to `axvo_procedure` / `axvo_method` reading the literal source symbol
(the MCM-correct token for a prefix-disciplined name, matching how
`axhems_scoped_method` already reads an implementation method name). It is an
*amendment* to two established voicings with many live consumers, not a fresh
mint, so it carries a real migration and is held for its own deliberate pace
rather than folded into guide authoring. Recorded here as ACGm_102's named
dependency; raised and deferred, not dropped.

## Related Guides

- **BCG** — host bash. Constant Discipline and Interface Contamination are the
  bash instances of the spine.
- **RCG** — host Rust. Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings), String Boundary
  Discipline, and Interface Contamination are the Rust instances.
- **WSG**, **CBG** — foreign-environment siblings (Windows transport, cloud step).
- **MCM** — builds the named homes ACG references; the design-time home.
- **AXLA** — the motif vocabulary; the lookahead scanning rule and the
  name-identity symbol-link dependency live against it.
- **CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" (mint)** — governs the naming homes
  themselves.

## Acronym Registry

| Term | Expansion |
|------|-----------|
| ACG | Allocation Coding Guide (this document) |
| The spine | "Reference the home" — values → constants, concepts → quoin-refs |
| Home | The one right location for a fact, fixed by when it is read (design / edit / execution time) |
| Move-type | A catalogued `ACGm_` cleanup move: detect-rule, authority, licensing, verifier, countable Done |
| mutate-now | Licensing: a wrong move is cheaply caught, so the verb may repair in place |
| detect-only | Licensing: the verb reads and reports; no repair until a verifier exists |
| Name-identity | Concept linkage where the implementing symbol *is* the link to its quoin |

lastPrompt: find the render command first but yes we should seed PCG in this chat.

content.text:
Three threads to close — the file sighting, the overlap verdict, and the robustness check.

**1. Yes, I can see it — but it's not in this clone.** `ACG-AllocationCodingGuide.md` landed in the **alpha clone** (`/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/`). That's why your `M` status shows it and my first Read (beta clone) missed it — another officium authored it over there, the multi-clone case the JJK discipline flags. I read it read-only and won't touch it (additive-only, not my file, different clone).

**2. PCG and ACG do *not* cover the same space — they're orthogonal.** Now grounded in the real doc, not the docket:
- **ACG (Allocation Coding Guide)** — "reference the home": where a *name/value/fact in our own source and specs* is allowed to live (values→constants, concepts→quoin-refs), the source-side complement to MCM. Audience: developers writing code/specs. A verifier-backed move catalog.
- **PCG (Procedure Curation Guide)** — crafting durable *IT operational runbooks* an unfamiliar human Executor follows during incidents. Audience: an AI helping an Editor write ops procedures. Guillemet substitution, single-path, verification-when-uncertain.

Different artifacts (our source/specs vs ops runbooks), different audiences, different failure mode (drift/duplication vs an Executor deviating). The only kinship is a faint rhyme — both lean on the "every line earns its presence" ethos, and both have a substitution-marker notion (ACG's quoin-refs vs PCG's `«…»`) — but that's one *principle* applied to two unrelated domains. **ACG does not supersede PCG.**

On PCG being cruft — separately true-ish, for a different reason: in rbm it's *dormant* (one incidental citation in the acronym-selection memo as a naming-pattern example, no live dependency). It's a standalone, intact lens in the cnmp library — just unwired. So: dormant lens, your call whether cnmp still wants it — but **not** redundant-with-ACG, which was the original worry. Changed nothing.

**3. Robustness check — yes, and firmer than my first answer.** The search earned its keep twice:
- **Confirmed the diagnosis in the wild** — the PlantUML forum reproduces our exact bug: `Queen's` → `Queenâ€™s` on POST while the same source renders clean via GET, even with the server reporting "Default Encoding: UTF-8." That's PUr_101, witnessed independently.
- **Corrected my fix.** My first instinct — add `Content-Type: charset=utf-8` to the POST — is the *weaker* answer: the forum shows the POST mojibake persisting and never confirms a header cures it. The robust path is PlantUML's **deflate-encoded URL transport** (UTF-8 → deflate → URL-safe ASCII), which is *"independent of HTTP charset negotiation"* by construction — it encodes *past* the neighbor's decode rather than trusting it. That's the Palisade-correct membrane, and it's what I wrote into PUG's `PUr_101` (charset-POST demoted to an unverified fallback).

So the **PUG seed is down**: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/PUG-PlantUmlGuide.md`, registered in the RBK acronym map beside CBG, founded on `PUr_101` with the robust transport as the membrane.

**Two coordination notes** (you run both chats):
- We minted in parallel — ACG (alpha/CMK) and PUG (beta/RBK) — without colliding: different letters, different kits, both homes defensible (ACG cross-cutting→CMK beside MCM; PUG render-specific→RBK beside CBG). Worth reconciling when the clones merge.
- ACG's "Done" calls for a *CLAUDE.md guide registry* that doesn't exist yet. When that work creates it, PUG should join, and the two should cross-reference each other's "Related Guides" line. I didn't pre-build it — that registry is the other officium's to shape.

Nothing committed — your tree has the new PUG file + the acronym edit, ready to notch when you want. If you'd like, the natural next step is the actual render fix (switch the `pluml` render case to the encoded-GET transport) + the mojibake gate + re-render the four SVGs — but that's RBK theurge work, separate from the `₣Bb` scrub, and I'd want to verify against the live `pluml` crucible before committing it.

Sources: [PlantUML Text Encoding](https://plantuml.com/text-encoding) · [Format for POSTing to plantuml server (forum)](https://forum.plantuml.net/18290/format-for-posting-to-plantuml-server)

content.text: Clean inventory: 15 em dashes (U+2014) and 1 en dash (U+2013), all used as spaced separators. Replacing both with an ASCII hyphen reads cleanly in every context (`Tier — Operator` → `Tier - Operator`, `15 min – 12 h` → `15 min - 12 h`). Doing the substitution and re-scanning to confirm the sources are now pure ASCII:

input.command:
python3 - <<'PYEOF'
import glob
total = 0
for f in sorted(glob.glob("diagrams/*.puml")):
    with open(f, encoding="utf-8") as fh:
        text = fh.read()
    n = text.count('—') + text.count('–')
    if n:
        text = text.replace('—', '-').replace('–', '-')
        with open(f, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as fh:
            fh.write(text)
        print(f"{f}: replaced {n} dash(es)")
        total += n
print(f"--- total replaced: {total} ---")

# Re-scan: confirm pure ASCII now
bad = 0
for f in sorted(glob.glob("diagrams/*.puml")):
    with open(f, encoding="utf-8") as fh:
        for ln, line in enumerate(fh, 1):
            for ch in line:
                if ord(ch) > 0x7F:
                    print(f"STILL NON-ASCII {f}:{ln} U+{ord(ch):04X}")
                    bad += 1
print(f"--- non-ASCII remaining: {bad} (0 = sources are pure ASCII) ---")
PYEOF
